summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the Golgi apparatus, how is it organized, and what are its functions in the cell?

Golgi apparatus (Golgi complex/body): (1) Discovered by Camillo Golgi (1898) using silver staining; Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine 1906 (shared with Ramón y Cajal). (2) Structure: stack of 4-8 flattened membrane cisternae. Polarized: cis face (receiving — near ER, receives transport vesicles from ER) → medial cisternae → trans face (shipping — facing plasma membrane, trans-Golgi network or TGN). (3) Functions: modification (O-linked glycosylation, modification of N-linked sugars from ER, phosphorylation — mannose-6-phosphate tag for lysosomal enzymes, sulfation, proteolytic processing — e.g., proinsulin → insulin), sorting (molecular address tags direct proteins to correct destinations), packaging (trans-Golgi network buds vesicles targeted to: plasma membrane for secretion or membrane insertion, lysosomes, or regulated secretory granules). (4) Vesicle trafficking: COPI vesicles (retrograde, Golgi → ER), COPII vesicles (anterograde, ER → Golgi), clathrin-coated vesicles (TGN → lysosomes, endosomes). James Rothman, Randy Schekman, Thomas Südhof — Nobel Prize 2013 for vesicle trafficking.

What is the endomembrane system, and how do its components work together to produce, modify, and deliver proteins?

The endomembrane system: a connected network of organelles related by direct physical continuity or vesicle transfer. Components: (1) Nuclear envelope: double membrane, continuous with rough ER, nuclear pores regulate traffic. (2) Rough ER: protein synthesis and initial modification. (3) Smooth ER: lipid synthesis, detox, Ca²⁺ storage. (4) Golgi apparatus: further modification, sorting, packaging. (5) Lysosomes: intracellular digestion. (6) Vesicles: transport containers between compartments. (7) Plasma membrane: receives vesicles via exocytosis, sends material in via endocytosis. The secretory pathway: nucleus (gene → mRNA) → RER (translation, folding, glycosylation) → COPII vesicles → Golgi cis face → medial Golgi → trans-Golgi network (sorting) → clathrin or secretory vesicles → destination. NOT part of endomembrane system: mitochondria, chloroplasts (have own DNA, double membranes from endosymbiosis — not derived from ER/Golgi), peroxisomes (may derive from ER but functionally independent). Nobel Prizes: Palade (1974 — secretory pathway), Rothman/Schekman/Südhof (2013 — vesicle trafficking).

What is the function of the large central vacuole in plant cells, and how does it compare to vacuoles in other cell types?

Vacuoles: membrane-bound storage compartments. (1) Plant central vacuole: large (up to 80-90% of cell volume), bounded by tonoplast membrane. Contents: water (turgor pressure), ions (K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺ for osmotic regulation), sugars, amino acids, organic acids (citric acid in citrus fruits), pigments (anthocyanins — red/blue/purple flower colors), defensive compounds (alkaloids, tannins — deter herbivores), waste products. Functions: turgor pressure (structural support in non-woody plants, drives cell expansion during growth), storage (nutrients, pigments, defensive chemicals), recycling (vacuolar enzymes break down proteins/organelles, similar to lysosomes), pH regulation (acidic vacuole pH maintained by H⁺ ATPase). (2) Food vacuoles: protists and phagocytic cells — formed by endocytosis → fuse with lysosomes → digest contents. (3) Contractile vacuoles: freshwater protists (Paramecium, Amoeba) — pump out excess water entering by osmosis → osmoregulation. (4) Animal cell vacuoles: smaller and more numerous — temporary storage.

What are ribosomes, where are they found, and what is their function in protein synthesis?

Ribosomes — protein synthesis machines: (1) Composition: ~60% rRNA + ~40% protein. NOT membrane-bound (not organelles in the strict sense). (2) Size: eukaryotic = 80S (60S large subunit + 40S small subunit). Prokaryotic/mitochondrial/chloroplast = 70S (50S + 30S). 'S' = Svedberg sedimentation coefficient (not additive). (3) Assembly: rRNA genes transcribed in nucleolus → rRNA combined with ribosomal proteins (imported from cytoplasm) → subunits assembled → exported through nuclear pores → join in cytoplasm during translation initiation. (4) Locations: free ribosomes (cytoplasm — make cytoplasmic, nuclear, peroxisomal, mitochondrial proteins), bound ribosomes (attached to rough ER via signal sequence/SRP pathway — make membrane, secretory, and lysosomal proteins). Same ribosome can be free or bound depending on the mRNA being translated. (5) Clinical: difference between 70S and 80S ribosomes exploited by antibiotics (tetracycline, erythromycin, chloramphenicol, streptomycin target 70S → kill bacteria without harming human 80S ribosomes — selective toxicity).

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Tay-Sachs disease — lysosomal storage disease: (1) Cause: autosomal recessive mutations in HEXA gene → deficient hexosaminidase A enzyme → cannot cleave GM2 ganglioside in lysosomes. (2) Pathology: GM2 ganglioside is a normal glycolipid component of neuronal membranes. Neurons continuously internalize and recycle membrane lipids → gangliosides delivered to lysosomes for degradation. Without hexosaminidase A → GM2 accumulates → lysosomes swell (membranous cytoplasmic bodies) → disrupts axonal transport, synaptic function, and triggers inflammation/apoptosis. (3) Why neurons? Highest ganglioside content of any cell type. Post-mitotic — cannot divide to dilute substrate. High metabolic rate. Long axons vulnerable to transport disruption. (4) Clinical: infantile form (most severe) — normal at birth → developmental regression at 3-6 months → seizures, blindness (cherry-red spot on retina), death by age 3-5. (5) Prevalence: Ashkenazi Jewish population (~1/30 carrier frequency). (6) This demonstrates that lysosomal function is not just 'garbage disposal' — it's essential for continuous cellular homeostasis. ~50 known lysosomal storage diseases collectively affect ~1/7,700 births.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A forensic scientist examines cells from an unknown tissue sample under an electron microscope. She observes abundant rough ER, a prominent Golgi apparatus with many transport vesicles, and numerous secretory granules near the plasma membrane. What type of cell is this most likely, and how do these organelle features relate to the cell's function?

Organelle profile reveals cell function: (1) Abundant RER + prominent Golgi + secretory granules = specialized secretory cell. The endomembrane system is highly developed to support massive protein production and export. (2) Example cells: Pancreatic acinar cells: secrete digestive enzymes (trypsinogen, amylase, lipase) → regulated exocytosis triggered by cholecystokinin (CCK). Plasma cells (differentiated B lymphocytes): secrete ~2,000 antibody molecules per second → extensive RER fills most of cytoplasm. Goblet cells: secrete mucus (glycoproteins). (3) Structure-function principle: cells with different functions have different organelle profiles. Secretory cells: abundant RER + Golgi. Steroid-secreting cells (adrenal cortex): abundant SER (steroid synthesis). Muscle cells: abundant mitochondria (ATP for contraction) + extensive SER (sarcoplasmic reticulum for Ca²⁺ storage). Phagocytic cells: abundant lysosomes. (4) EM observation: George Palade's Nobel Prize-winning work (1974) used EM to trace the secretory pathway through RER → Golgi → secretory vesicles → exocytosis in pancreatic acinar cells.

Example: The 2016 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Yoshinori Ohsumi for his work on autophagy — the process by which cells 'eat themselves' by enclosing damaged organelles in double-membrane vesicles (autophagosomes) that then fuse with lysosomes for digestion and recycling. Why is autophagy essential for cellular health, and what happens when it malfunctions?

Autophagy — Nobel Prize 2016 (Yoshinori Ohsumi): (1) Process: isolation membrane (phagophore) forms → elongates around target → closes to form autophagosome (double-membrane vesicle) → autophagosome fuses with lysosome → autolysosome → lysosomal enzymes digest contents → building blocks (amino acids, fatty acids, nucleotides) recycled to cytoplasm. Key proteins: ATG (autophagy-related) family — Ohsumi identified 15 essential ATG genes in yeast. (2) Types: macroautophagy (bulk — entire organelles), selective autophagy: mitophagy (damaged mitochondria), pexophagy (peroxisomes), ribophagy (ribosomes), aggrephagy (protein aggregates), xenophagy (intracellular pathogens). (3) Functions: quality control (remove damaged organelles, prevent ROS accumulation), nutrient recycling (during starvation — sustains cell until nutrients available), development (cellular remodeling), immunity (xenophagy destroys intracellular bacteria like M. tuberculosis). (4) Disease links: defective autophagy → neurodegenerative diseases (protein aggregates: Aβ/tau in Alzheimer's, α-synuclein in Parkinson's, huntingtin in Huntington's) → cancer (loss of autophagy → genomic instability → transformation; but established tumors may use autophagy for survival under nutrient stress) → accelerated aging → susceptibility to infections. (5) Therapeutic potential: rapamycin (mTOR inhibitor) enhances autophagy → extends lifespan in model organisms; autophagy modulation being explored for cancer, neurodegeneration, and anti-aging.
